slots.text: Most founders validate ideas by asking 'would you use this?' Wrong question. Ask 'how do you solve this problem today?' Then watch them describe their current painful workaround.

3 things that kill user interviews:
1. Leading questions that beg for yes
2. Talking about your solution too early
3. Not asking for the story of their last bad experience

Building with AI feels like having a really smart intern who never asks clarifying questions. You get exactly what you asked for, not what you actually needed. The gap between those two things is where most AI projects die.
Founders who ship fast usually have one thing in common: they cut scope ruthlessly before they start building, not after they realize they're behind schedule.
Every workflow friction point you ignore becomes a user drop-off point. There's no such thing as 'just one more click' when people have alternatives.

4 signs you're validating wrong:
1. Everyone says yes but no one signs up
2. You're explaining more than listening
3. Friends are your primary data source
4. You haven't heard a clear no yet

Most idea validation feels like checking boxes instead of actually listening. You're not looking for people to say yes to your idea. You're looking for people who already have the problem badly enough to pay for a terrible solution.

What actually kills user interviews:

1. Leading questions that fish for validation
2. Talking to people who are too polite to be honest
3. Asking hypotheticals instead of past behavior
4. Taking notes instead of recording
5. Skipping the follow-up

The hardest part about building with AI isn't the technical stuff. It's that everyone expects it to be magic, but you still need to understand the actual workflow you're trying to improve. AI that automates bad processes just makes bad processes faster.

Founders who talk to 50 users and pivot are more successful than founders who talk to 5 users and stay the course. But most founders would rather build for 6 months than have 20 awkward conversations.
Friction isn't always bad. Sometimes friction is the only thing preventing your users from making expensive mistakes. The goal isn't to remove all friction—it's to remove the right friction.

Signs you're solving a real problem:

1. People complain about it without prompting
2. They've already tried 3+ solutions that suck
3. They've built internal workarounds
4. They can tell you exactly how much it costs them
5. They ask when they can start using yours
Most founders skip the boring parts of user research. The boring parts are where you find out your product actually sucks.

Three signs you're validating wrong:

1. Everyone says "great idea" but won't pay
2. You're asking hypotheticals instead of studying behavior
3. Your feedback comes from people who look exactly like you

The AI tools that actually stick in my workflow are the ones that remove steps, not add capabilities. But most AI features feel like someone bolted a chatbot onto existing software and called it innovation.
Founders who talk to 5 users think they need more features. Founders who talk to 50 users realize they need fewer.
Your workflow has friction you don't notice because you built muscle memory around broken things. Fresh eyes see what you've gone blind to.

What kills good ideas:

1. Building for imaginary users
2. Optimizing for edge cases first
3. Adding complexity to feel productive
4. Mistaking activity for progress
Most founders confuse user feedback with user requests. Feedback tells you what's broken. Requests tell you what they think would fix it. Listen to the first, be skeptical of the second.

3 validation red flags that took me too long to learn:

1. Users say "interesting" instead of asking when they can use it
2. They ask about features before understanding the core problem
3. They want to "think about it" after seeing the demo

The hardest part of AI integration isn't the technical implementation. It's figuring out which decisions you want humans to make vs which ones you want to automate. Most founders automate the wrong things first.
Workflow friction usually lives in the handoffs between tools, not within the tools themselves. Users adapt to clunky interfaces faster than they adapt to switching contexts.
Talking to users feels awkward because you're trying to extract insights instead of having conversations. Stop mining for gold and start actually listening.

Signs you're building the right thing:

1. Users describe the problem without you asking
2. They've already tried to solve it themselves
3. They ask about pricing before you mention it
4. They introduce you to others with the same problem
Most founders think user interviews are about asking what people want. Wrong. They're about watching what people actually do when they think nobody's judging them.

Three things that kill idea validation:

1. Leading questions that telegraph the answer you want
2. Talking to friends who will lie to be nice
3. Confusing polite interest with actual demand

The hardest part about building with AI isn't the technical integration. It's figuring out which human decisions you should automate vs which ones you should augment. Get this wrong and users feel replaced instead of empowered.
People will endure incredible friction if the outcome matters enough to them. But they'll abandon a product over tiny inconveniences if they're just browsing. Context determines tolerance.
Your workflow has too many steps if you find yourself making mental notes to 'clean this up later.' Users won't forgive what you've already gotten used to ignoring.

Signs you're talking to the wrong users:

1. They keep suggesting features for 'other people'
2. Their problems sound theoretical, not urgent
3. They have time for hour-long calls about your idea

Most founders think user interviews are about asking "would you buy this?" Wrong question. Ask "walk me through the last time you dealt with [problem]." The story tells you everything the hypothetical won't.

Signs your idea validation is actually just confirmation bias:

1. You're only talking to people who already agree with you
2. Leading questions that telegraph the "right" answer
3. Interpreting polite interest as demand
4. Skipping the "how do you solve this today" question

The hardest part about building with AI isn't the technical integration. It's figuring out which manual processes actually need automation vs which ones people want to keep doing themselves. Users say they want everything automated but their behavior says otherwise.

Friction in workflows is often intentional, not accidental. That approval step that seems redundant? It's probably catching edge cases or forcing someone to actually think. Remove the wrong friction and you create bigger problems.

When someone says "this is exactly what we need" in a user interview, dig deeper. What they usually mean is "this sounds good in theory." The real test is whether they'll change their current workflow to use it.

What actually happens when you ship an MVP:

1. Half your assumptions were wrong
2. Users find workarounds you never considered
3. The feature you thought was nice-to-have becomes essential
4. The core value prop needs rewording, not rebuilding
Most founders skip validation because they think it means asking "would you buy this?" That question is useless. Better question: "walk me through the last time this problem cost you time or money."

Why user interviews fail:
1. You lead them to your solution
2. You ask hypotheticals instead of past behavior
3. You talk to people who are polite, not desperate
4. You stop after 5 conversations

AI tools are making it easier to build the wrong thing faster. You can spin up an MVP in hours now, but validation still takes weeks of actual conversations. The bottleneck shifted from building to understanding.
Founders who say "users don't know what they want" usually mean "users don't want what I built." The ones who consistently nail product-market fit spend more time listening than explaining.
Friction isn't always bad. Sometimes it's the only thing preventing your users from making expensive mistakes. Remove the right friction, keep the guardrails.

Signs you're building a vitamin, not a painkiller:
1. Users say "that's cool" instead of "when can I get this"
2. They forget to follow up after demos
3. You're explaining the problem more than the solution
